coredata.dc:title: Preparation of cellulose-derived carbon dots and their applications in removal and detection of silver ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Xiangzhi
Wang, Donghan
Shen, Fei
Hou, Juan
coredata.dc:description: Silver and its compounds are widely used in various industries, but their toxicity and bioaccumulation pose serious environmental and health risks. Current methods for Ag+ detection are limited by high costs, complex operations and poor selectivity. In this work, cellulose-derived carbon dots (CPCDs) were synthesized from renewable carboxymethyl cellulose and o-phenylenediamine via a simple, green, and low-temperature method. The resulting CPCDs exhibited excellent fluorescence properties and high sensitivity to Ag+. The fluorescence could be quenched in the presence of Ag+ in the range of 0–200 μM, with a low detection limit of 0.01 μM. The method was successfully applied to real water samples, achieving high recovery rates (96.97–104.75 %) and low RSD (1.51–5.66 %). Additionally, CPCDs also demonstrated good Ag+ adsorption capability. Adsorption reached equilibrium within 60 min, with a maximum adsorption capacity of 710.2 mg g−1. The adsorption process followed a pseudo-second-order kinetic model and fitted well with the Freundlich isotherm, suggesting multilayer adsorption on a heterogeneous surface through surface coordination and redox-induced aggregation. This work presents a sustainable and cost-effective nanomaterial with integrated sensing and remediation capabilities, offering strong potential for practical applications in environmental monitoring and water purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose
Carbon dots
Fluorescence detection
Ag+
Removal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study on adsorption performance of Ag+
Detection of Ag+ by fluorescence quenching
Adsorption properties of CPCDs for Ag+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the support from the Joint Fund Project of the Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (YDZJ202401554ZYTS). The authors also would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (http://www.shiyanjia.com) for the assistance with testing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the CPCDs; (b) HRTEM image of the CPCDs; (c) the size distribution based on statistical analysis of 100 particles; (d) FTIR spectrum of CPCDs; the full XPS spectrum of CPCDs (e) and the high-resolution spectra of C 1s (f), N 1s (g), and O 1s (h).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths of CPCDs. (b) fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of CPCDs, insets are the photographs of CPCDs solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV beam (right), respectively. (c) UV–Vis spectrum of CPCDs. (d) Lifetime decay profiles of CPCDs.

Different metal ions (a), small organic molecules (b), and anions (c) on the F/F0 of CPCDs, (d) Photographs of CPCDs with different metal ions under 365 nm UV light; (e) Fluorescence spectra of the CPCDs with Ag+ from 0 to 200 μM; (f) the linear relationship between F/F0 and the concentration of Ag+.

(a) Fluorescence decay curves of CPCDs with different Ag+ concentrations; (b) Stern-Volmer plot analysis of F0/F with concentration of Ag+; (c) Zeta potentials of CPCDs, CPCDs + 100 μM Ag+, and CPCDs + 200 μM Ag+; (d) FTIR spectra of CPCDs (i), CPCDs + 100 μM Ag+ (ii), and CPCDs + 200 μM Ag+ (iii); The full XPS spectrum of CPCDs + Ag+ system (e) and the high-resolution spectra of C 1s (f), N1s (g), O 1s (h) and Ag 3d (i).

(a) Plot of the relationship between initial Ag+ concentration and adsorption capacity; (b) adsorption isotherms fitted by Langmuir and Freundlich models; Adsorption kinetics fitted by pseudo-first-order (c) and pseudo-second-order models (d).
Preparation process of CPCDs and their application in removal and detection of Ag+.
Detection of Ag+ in real water samples.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection.

row.entry: boric acid, o-phenylenediamine, PMA, APS, Bis, TEMED

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different adsorbents for Ag+ adsorption.

row.entry: 1,3,5-triethynylbenzene + 1,4-dibromobenzene + Pd (0) + CuI + DMF + Et3N → stirred at 80 °C for 72 h in Ar atmosphere → soxhlet extraction with CH3OH for 24 h → dried at 65 °C for 24 h
SBA-15 → warmed 2 h under Ar atmosphere → +dried toluene → stirred for 30 min → +N-methyl-N’-propyltrimethoxysilylimidazolium chloride → stirred for 48 h under Ar atmosphere
degummed silk + formic acid-calcium chloride solution → methanol bath
CMCDAS + DI water → stirred for 0.5 h at 25 °C under nitrogen → +ammonium persulfate → stirred for 15 min → +AA + MBA → stirred and refluxed at 70 °C for 3 h
acrylate modified CAB + TMPTA + cysteine → stirred at 500 rpm for 5 min under N2 atmosphere → +IRGACURE 2022 → UV rays for 3 min
FeCl2·4H2O + FeCl3·6H2O + HCl → separated → +water → heated to 80 °C → +sodium silicate + HCl → stirred 3 h → +toluene + 2-MTPS → refluxed for 24 h
rhodanine-3-acetic acid + p-phthalaldehyde + acetic acid + ammonium acetate → refluxed at 373 K for 48 h + ZrCl4 + DMF → 413 K for 72 h
CMC + OPD + 50 ml DI water → stirred at 20 °C for 24 h

head.ce:title: Preparation of cellulose-derived carbon dots and their applications in removal and detection of silver ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Environmental Engineering, Jilin Provincial International Joint Research Center of Photo-functional Materials and Chemistry, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jilin Provincial International Joint Research Center of Photo-functional Materials and Chemistry
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Environmental Engineering, Jilin Provincial International Joint Research Center of Photo-functional Materials and Chemistry, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CPCDs were prepared for sensitive detection and efficient removal of Ag+, offering a dual-functional approach for environmental monitoring and pollution control.

Silver and its compounds are widely used in various industries, but their toxicity and bioaccumulation pose serious environmental and health risks. Current methods for Ag+ detection are limited by high costs, complex operations and poor selectivity. In this work, cellulose-derived carbon dots (CPCDs) were synthesized from renewable carboxymethyl cellulose and o-phenylenediamine via a simple, green, and low-temperature method. The resulting CPCDs exhibited excellent fluorescence properties and high sensitivity to Ag+. The fluorescence could be quenched in the presence of Ag+ in the range of 0–200 μM, with a low detection limit of 0.01 μM. The method was successfully applied to real water samples, achieving high recovery rates (96.97–104.75 %) and low RSD (1.51–5.66 %). Additionally, CPCDs also demonstrated good Ag+ adsorption capability. Adsorption reached equilibrium within 60 min, with a maximum adsorption capacity of 710.2 mg g−1. The adsorption process followed a pseudo-second-order kinetic model and fitted well with the Freundlich isotherm, suggesting multilayer adsorption on a heterogeneous surface through surface coordination and redox-induced aggregation. This work presents a sustainable and cost-effective nanomaterial with integrated sensing and remediation capabilities, offering strong potential for practical applications in environmental monitoring and water purification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets used or analyzed in this work are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industrialization, silver and its compounds have been widely used in medical, electronics, and water treatment industries due to their excellent antibacterial and conductive properties [1]. However, their extensive release, particularly through wastewater discharge and improper disposal of silver-containing products, has raised significant environmental and ecological concerns. Research has demonstrated that high concentrations of Ag+ are toxic to aquatic organisms, and can affect growth, reproduction, and physiological functions [2]. Moreover, prolonged exposure to Ag+ in humans is also harmful to immune, nervous, and reproductive systems. Therefore, the development of efficient detection and removal of Ag+ is of great importance for environmental monitoring and public health protection.

Traditional detection methods for Ag+ include atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [3], liquid chromatography (LC) [4], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [5], and atomic emission spectrometry (AES) [6]. These techniques offer high sensitivity and accuracy, but there are some limitations, such as requiring expensive instruments, skilled operators, and complex sample pretreatment procedures. While electrochemical sensors demonstrate simpler and faster analysis, they are often susceptible to interference from other ions present in the sample, resulting in poor selectivity and accuracy [7]. Fluorescence-based detection methods have gained significant attention for their advantages, such as rapid response, high sensitivity, simplicity, and the potential for visual detection without the need for sophisticated instrumentation [8]. Among the various fluorescent materials available, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as an attractive option due to their unique optical properties, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and ease of functionalization [9].

In recent years, biomass-derived materials for synthesizing CDs have attracted considerable interest due to their sustainability, environmental compatibility and cost-effectiveness. Cellulose, the most abundant natural polymer, is a promising raw material for CDs synthesis [10]. It is widely sourced from renewable and readily available resources such as wood, cotton, and agricultural waste [11]. The numerous organic functional groups facilitate effective coordination with metal ions. The use of cellulose-derived CDs not only reduces the environmental impact associated with conventional synthetic route, but also promotes the utilization of biomass waste and contributes to a circular economy [12]. For example, Zhang et al. [13] synthesized blue-green emissive polymer dots for the sensitive detection of tetracycline. Wan et al. [14] prepared N-CDs based fluorescence sensor using melamine and sodium carboxymethyl cellulose as precursors. Incorporation into filter paper and gel enabled portable tracking of Fe3+. However, current cellulose-derived CDs typically exhibited a single-function of fluorescence response. To further enhance their utility, combining both detection and removal functions within a single process would offer significant environmental advantages. Therefore, rational surface engineering of CDs could promote fluorescence response and enable aggregation-induced metal ion capture, which would offer significant innovative potential in environmental sensing and remediation.

In this study, we presented a simple, cost-effective and eco-friendly approach for the synthesis of cellulose-derived CDs (CPCDs). The CPCDs exhibited bright yellow fluorescence and showed high selectivity toward Ag+. The fluorescence intensity of CPCDs was gradually quenched with increasing Ag+ concentrations over a range of 0–200 μM, allowing for sensitive and selective detection. The satisfactory results from real water sample analysis indicated the applicability and feasibility. Notably, the CPCDs also displayed good adsorption capability for Ag+, which facilitated simultaneous monitoring and remediation. This work not only advances the functional performance of biomass-derived CDs but also promotes the use of renewable materials in developing sustainable environmental technologies.

O-phenylenediamine (OPD) and cadmium sulfate (CdSO4) were obtained from Rhon Reagents. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), MnCl2·4H2O, Na2S2O3, urea, glucose, ascorbic acid, glutathione and amino acids were purchased from Macklin. Metal ion salts, including AlCl3·6H2O, KCl, MgCl2, NaCl, CaCl2, FeCl3·3H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, BaSO4, CuSO4, Pb(NO3)2, AgNO3, HgNO3, NaBr, NaF, Na2NO3, Na3PO4, Na2SO4 and NaI, were acquired from Aladdin. Na2CO3 was sourced from Bidder Pharmaceutical. ZnSO4 was obtained from Shanghai Reagents. All chemicals were of analytical grade, and used as received without further purification. Deionized water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm was produced using a JL-RO100 Millipore-Q Plus water purifier.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA) operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. UV–vis spectra were recorded using a UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on a K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected using an FTIR-600 spectrometer (SK, Wuhan, China) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. Zeta potential was measured using a NANOTRAC WAVE II instrument (Microtrac, USA). Fluorescence lifetimes and quantum yield were determined with a FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a F97Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Leng Guang, Shanghai, China) with slit widths of 10 nm for both emission and excitation spectra.

OPD and CMC were mixed at a mass ratio of 1:1.5 and added to 50 mL DI water at pH = 7. The mixture was stirred at 20 °C for 24 h, resulting in a yellow solution (Scheme 1 ). The solution was then centrifuged and filtered to obtain a purified CPCDs solution. After freeze-drying, CPCDs powder was obtained for subsequent characterization and analysis. To optimize the preparation conditions, fluorescence quantum yield (QY) measurements were performed to assess the effects of various factors, including raw material mass ratios, pH, and temperatures, on the QY of the CPCDs.

In addition, control experiments were conducted to investigate the role of CMC in the synthesis process, including synthesizing CDs without CMC and replacing CMC with glucose or citric acid under identical synthetic conditions.

To evaluate the sensitivity of CPCDs for Ag+ detection, various concentrations of Ag+ were introduced into the CPCDs solution (pH = 7) in a quartz cuvette. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded from 440 to 800 nm with an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The excitation and emission slit widths were set to 10 nm. To assess the specificity of the CPCDs-based sensing system for Ag+, the fluorescence response was evaluated under the same experimental conditions in the presence of various metal ions, anions, and small molecules.

Real water samples were collected from laboratory tap water, Yitong River (Changchun, Jilin Province, China), and South Lake (Changchun, Jilin Province, China). The samples were filtered through a 0.45 μm microporous membrane to remove particulate matter and stored in clean bottles. The concentrations of Ag+ were measured using both ICP-MS and the proposed method. To evaluate the recovery, various concentrations of standard Ag+ solutions were spiked into water samples, and the spiked recoveries were calculated to assess the accuracy of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on adsorption performance of Ag+

ce:section.ce:para:
Isothermal adsorption experiments were carried out in a thermostatic shaker at 25 °C. CPCDs (30 mg) were dispersed in 10 mL of Ag+ standard solutions with initial concentrations ranging from 0 to 40 g L−1 at pH = 7.0. After 12 h of equilibrium, the supernatant was collected by centrifugation and filtration, and the concentration of residual Ag+ was analyzed by ICP-MS. The adsorption capacity (Qe, mg g−1) was calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
Q e
=
(
c 0
-
c e
) × V
m
where c0 and ce (mg/L) are the initial and equilibrium concentrations of Ag+, respectively; V (mL) is the solution volume; and m (mg) is the mass of CPCDs. The equilibrium data were fitted using the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models, expressed as Eqs. (2) and (3):
(2)
Q e
=
Q
max
K L
c e
1 + K
L
c e
(3)
Q e
=
K F
c
e
1 n
where Qmax (mg g−1) represents the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity, KL (L mg−1) is the Langmuir constant; KF and n are Freundlich constants indicative of adsorption intensity and heterogeneity.

Kinetic experiments were conducted by adding 90 mg of CPCDs to 30 mL of 300 mg L−1 Ag+ solution. At predetermined time intervals (1–120 min), the supernatant was collected by centrifugation and filtration, and the concentration of residual Ag+ was analyzed by ICP-MS. The adsorption kinetics were modeled using pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order equations, as given in Eqs. (4) and (5):
(4)
Q t
=
Q e
( 1 -
e
-
K 1
t
)
(5)
t
Q t
=
Q
e
2
K 2
+
t
Q e
where t is adsorption time (min); Qt and Qe (mg g−1) are the adsorption capacities at time t and at equilibrium, respectively; K1 (min−1) and K2 (g mg−1 min−1) are the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order rate constants, respectively.

To better understand the role of cellulose in the synthesis of CPCDs and their performance in Ag+ detection and removal, we conducted several control experiments. Firstly, when CMC was excluded and only OPD was used, no CDs were formed. Under the same experimental conditions, CMC was replaced with glucose and citric acid to synthesize Glu-CDs and CA-CDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1a, both Glu-CDs and CA-CDs exhibited very weak fluorescence. Upon the addition of Ag+, the fluorescence response remained minimal (Fig. S1c). Ag+ did not exhibit significant adsorption or aggregation in Glu-CDs and CA-CDs solutions (Fig. S1b). Therefore, CMC shows critical effect on surface functionalities, which facilitates strong fluorescence response and adsorption properties.

Subsequently, the preparation conditions for CPCDs were optimized based on fluorescence quantum yield (QY), as it influenced sensitivity and efficiency in fluorescence-based detection. As illustrated in Fig. S2a, the mass ratio of OPD and CMC was optimized at 1:1.5. OPD served as the core precursor for the formation of CPCDs, while CMC with functional carboxyl groups, enhanced the surface properties. A higher amount of CMC led to increased surface passivation, reducing the QY. A lower ratio resulted in insufficient functionalization and weaker fluorescence signal. The pH value of synthetic system may affect the protonation and deprotonation of CMC surface groups, thereby influencing the stability and reaction with OPD (Fig. S2b). At neutral pH, the functional groups on CMC remained stable, allowing for efficient interaction with OPD. Extreme pH values (acidic or alkaline) affect the charge distribution and hydrophilicity of CMC, thereby decreasing the QY. pH 7 was found to be optimal for CPCDs synthesis. The effect of reaction temperature was demonstrated in Fig. S2c. At lower temperature (e.g., 4°C), extending the reaction time did not significantly improve the QY. While higher temperatures (e.g., 40°C) may lead to aggregation or decomposition of the CPCDs and reduce the QY. Therefore, the reaction mixture was stirred at room temperature (20°C) for 24 h, which ensured a mild, controlled environment that minimized thermal degradation of the products.

In summary, the optimized conditions—OPD to CMC ratio of 1:1.5, pH 7, reaction time of 24 h, and a temperature of 20°C—yielded the highest QY (5.85 %) for CPCDs. These conditions effectively balanced the formation, stability, and fluorescence efficiency, making them ideal for subsequent analytical applications.

The morphology, size, and structure of the prepared CPCDs were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in the TEM images, the CPCDs are spherical nanoparticles with a uniform distribution ( Fig. 1
a). Statistical analysis of 100 CPCDs indicates that the sizes range from 1.25 to 4.25 nm, with an average particle size of 2.75 nm (Fig. 1 c). The HRTEM image reveals good crystallinity of CPCDs, with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.23 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphite (Fig. 1 b) [15].

The FTIR spectrum of CPCDs revealed multiple functional groups on their surface (Fig. 1 d). Broad adsorption bands at around 3050–3600 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H groups. The strong absorption band at 1620 cm−1 is mainly due to C=O stretching of amide or carboxyl groups. The peak at 1503 cm−1 is originated from C=N or Schiff base stretching. The sharp band at 1385 cm−1 is characteristic of methyl bending. Peaks at 1324 cm−1 and 1274 cm−1 are assigned to C–N stretching vibration in heterocycles or conjugated systems, and N–H/O–H bending vibrations. The prominent peaks at around 1045 cm−1 is ascribed to C–O stretching. The small band at 740 cm−1 is due to out-of-plane bending of aromatic C–H groups, further confirming the aromatic nature of the carbon core. These results indicate the presence of amine, hydroxyl, and other oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CPCDs [13,16–18]. Therefore, the CPCDs exhibit good solubility in water. XPS analysis was performed to further investigate the surface composition of CPCDs. The overall XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1 e) reveals three prominent peaks at binding energies of 283.81 eV, 401.90 eV, and 531.43 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. Deconvolution of the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1 f) reveals five peaks at 282.4 eV, 283.6 eV, 284.3 eV, 285.0 eV and 286.1 eV, which are assigned to the C=C/C–C, C–N, C=N, C–O and C=O groups, respectively. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1 g) shows two peaks at 397.60 eV and 405.51 eV, corresponding to graphite N and N–O groups, respectively. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1 h) exhibits two peaks at 529.61 eV and 531.13 eV, which are attributed to C–O and C=O groups. These results confirm the successful functionalization of the CPCDs with various oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups, enhancing their potential for various applications [13,19–21].

The CPCDs solution appears pale yellow under daylight illumination and emits bright yellow fluorescence when exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp (as shown in the inset of Fig. 2
a). The emission spectra are recorded at various excitation wavelengths (ranging from 300 nm to 600 nm in 20 nm intervals) (Fig. 2 b). When the excitation wavelength was set at 420 nm, the maximum emission occurred at 565 nm. Changes in the excitation wavelength results in variations in emission intensity, but no shift in the emission wavelength can be observed. The excitation-independent emission properties indicate that the obtained CPCDs exhibit relatively uniform size and surface characteristics [22].

The UV–Vis spectrum of CPCDs (Fig. 2 c) shows two distinct absorption peaks at 290 nm and 420 nm, corresponding to the π-π* electronic transition of C=C bonds and the n-π* electronic transition of C–N or C=O bonds, respectively [23]. The fluorescence lifetime (τ) was measured using fluorescence decay curve at Ex=420 nm. (Fig. 2 d). The average fluorescence lifetime of CPCDs was calculated to be 1.79 ns.

In order to evaluate the stability of CPCDs in aqueous solution, fluorescence intensity was monitored under varying concentrations of NaCl (0–2 M) and exposure to 365 nm UV light for 120 min (Fig. S3). The fluorescence intensity remained stable across the range of NaCl concentrations (0–2 mol/L), and there was no significant decrease after 120 min fluorescence under UV irradiation. These results demonstrate that the CPCDs exhibit excellent photostability, making them suitable for long-term analyte detection [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ by fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: In order to optimize the detection conditions for Ag+, the effects of pH, buffer solution and reaction time on fluorescence quenching efficiency were investigated. The highest fluorescence quenching efficiency was observed in deionized water (Fig. S4a and b). It was speculated that the high ionic strength of buffers may induce competitive binding and cause CPCDs to preferentially bind with ions in the buffer solution. Thus, the detection sensitivity was reduced. With the increase of pH, the fluorescence quenching efficiency firstly increases and then decreases, reaching its maximum at pH 7 (Fig. S4c and d). In acidic conditions, protonation of the functional groups on the surface of CPCDs decreases their reaction with Ag+. In alkaline conditions, Ag+ tends to hydrolyze and precipitate, resulting in lower quenching efficiency. The fluorescence intensity of CPCDs was rapidly quenched upon the addition of Ag+, and it stabilized after 1 min of reaction (Fig. S4e and f). Therefore, in subsequent experiments, the solution pH was set to 7, and the CPCDs solution was incubated with Ag+ for 1 min before testing.

Under the optimized detection conditions, 15 metal ions, 9 anions and 14 organic small molecules at the same concentrations of 150 μM, were introduced to the CPCDs solutions. The fluorescence spectra were subsequently recorded in Fig. 3
a–c. Only the addition of Ag+ induced a significant fluorescence quenching, while other ions and molecules had minimal impact on the fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, under 365 nm UV light irradiation, the CPCDs solution emitted bright yellow fluorescence in the absence of Ag+, whereas fluorescence quenching occurred in the presence of Ag+ (Fig. 3 d). Therefore, the CPCDs exhibited high selectivity for Ag+ and could be effectively used as a fluorescent probe for Ag+ detection.

As the concentration of Ag+ increases, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases, as illustrated in Fig. 3 e. Within the concentration range of 0–200 μM, F/F0 showed a strong linear correlation with Ag+ concentration (Fig. 3 f). The linear regression equation was as follows:
F/F0 = 1.0117–0.00436CAg +
where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of Ag+, respectively. The correlation coefficient R2 was found to be 0.997. The detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 0.01 μM based on 3σ/KSV.

The interaction mechanism between Ag+ and CPCDs was systematically investigated. Firstly, the fluorescence lifetime of CPCDs exhibited negligible changes upon increasing Ag+ concentrations (0–250 μM), suggesting that the quenching mechanism was predominantly static rather than dynamic (Fig. 4
a). This conclusion was further supported by Stern–Volmer equation:
F0/F = 1 + KSV[Ag+] = 1 + Kqτ0[Ag+]
where F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Ag+, respectively. The Stern-Volmer constant KSV was determined from linear fitting, and the fluorescence lifetime τ0 of CPCDs (estimated to be 1.79 × 10−9 s) (Fig. 4 b). The calculated quenching rate constant (Kq) exceeded the maximum diffusion-controlled quenching constant (∼2.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1). Thus, the fluorescence quenching of CPCDs by Ag+ followed a static quenching mechanism, rather than a dynamic (collisional) mechanism. Moreover, UV–Vis absorption spectra of Ag+ showed no overlap with the excitation or emission spectra of CPCDs (Fig. S5), excluding Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) as primary quenching pathways. Zeta potential measurements revealed an increase in surface charge after Ag+ addition, indicating that positively charged Ag+ were electrostatically adsorbed onto the negatively charged CPCDs surface, inducing particle aggregation (Fig. 4 c).

To further investigate surface chemical interactions, FTIR and XPS analyses were conducted. In Fig. 4 d, FTIR spectra demonstrated that the characteristic bands corresponding to N–H stretching and C=N vibrations diminished and eventually disappeared with increasing Ag+ concentration. Simultaneously, a new absorption peak at 1530 cm−1 emerged, assigned to asymmetric stretching of nitro groups (–NO2). It is speculated that surface amino and imine groups of CPCDs were oxidized by Ag+ into N–O/N=O species. Additionally, the carboxymethyl bending vibration at 1459 cm−1 also gradually disappeared, and the C=O stretching band at 1620 cm−1 showed a slight blue shift, which was consistent with Ag+ coordination to carbonyl groups. The aromatic C–H out-of-plane bending at 740 cm−1 also weakened, suggesting partial disruption of aromatic domains. These changes confirm that Ag+ induces both redox reactions and surface coordination, leading to structural reconstruction of CPCDs surface. XPS analysis further confirmed these interactions ( Fig. 4 e). In the C 1s spectrum, the intensity of the C=N component decreased, and the C=O peak disappeared after Ag+ addition, indicating chemical transformation of imine and carbonyl functionalities ( Fig. 4 f). The N 1s spectrum showed an increase in N–O species and a decrease in graphitic N, further confirming oxidative modification of nitrogen-containing groups ( Fig. 4 g). Similarly, the O 1s spectrum displayed the loss of C=O signal, indicating the involvement of carbonyl groups in Ag+-induced redox or coordination processes ( Fig. 4 h). Notably, the Ag 3d high-resolution spectrum showed two characteristic peaks at 365.95 eV (Ag 3d5/2) and 371.96 eV (Ag 3d3/2), consistent with the presence of metallic silver (Ag0) ( Fig. 4 i). [25]. Thus, Ag0 was partially reduced in situ on the CPCDs surface.

TEM images revealed that CPCDs alone were well dispersed (Fig. 1 a), while the addition of Ag+ resulted in significant aggregation and the formation of spherical silver nanoparticles (Ag0) with diameters ranging from 40 to 60 nm (Fig. S6). This indicated the dual roles of Ag+ in surface interaction and in situ reduction. As evidence, the XRD pattern in Fig. S7 showed a broad diffraction peak around 2θ = 24°, corresponding to amorphous carbon (CPCDs). Distinct diffraction peaks at 38.116°, 44.277°, 64.426°, and 77.472° matched the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of face-centered cubic silver (fcc Ag), respectively, in accordance with PDF#04-0783 (Silver-3C, syn-Ag). These results confirm the crystalline nature of AgNPs formed on the CPCDs surface. Furthermore, upon the gradual addition of Ag+ (0–180 μM) to CPCDs solution, the UV–Vis absorption spectra exhibited an enhanced peak at ∼420 nm, which is assigned to Ag0 formation (Fig. S8a). Visible color change from pale yellow to brown was observed, accompanied by slight precipitation at higher concentrations (Fig. S8b).

Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of CPCDs by Ag+ involves a synergistic mechanism of static quenching and aggregation-induced effects. Ag+ was firstly electrostatically adsorbed onto the CPCDs surface and then coordinates with oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups, disrupting surface passivation and promoting aggregation. Meanwhile, in situ reduction of Ag+ by surface amino or imine groups led to the nucleation and growth of Ag0 nanoparticles, contributing to structural and optical change. This dual interaction—comprising chemical surface reconstruction and nanoparticle-induced aggregation—collectively resulted in efficient fluorescence quenching.

In order to investigate the practicability and feasibility in real sample detection, the detection of Ag+ in tap water, Yitong River, and South Lake samples was performed. As shown in Table 1 , no Ag+ was detected in the water samples by our proposed method and ICP-MS. The spiked recoveries were obtained ranging from 96.97 % to 104.75 %, demonstrating good accuracy and reliability of the proposed method. The relative standard deviation (RSD) values were within the acceptable range of 1.51 % to 5.66 % (n = 5), further confirming the precision and reproducibility of the method. Table 2 compares the performance of our proposed method with other reported CDs-based probes on the detection of Ag+. The proposed method offers several advantages, including high sensitivity, broader detection range, superior selectivity and comparable detection limit, making it more reliable for practical applications. Furthermore, the method is cost-effective and simple, with minimal sample preparation and a shorter detection time compared to other methods. These attributes make the proposed method a promising tool for environmental monitoring and water quality analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption properties of CPCDs for Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: In the course of Ag+ detection experiments, we initially observed a significant adsorption phenomenon when CPCDs were introduced into Ag+ solutions (Fig. S9a). Comparative adsorption experiments were conducted using a series of metal ions (Fe3+, Co2+, K+, Na+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+) under identical conditions (Fig. S9b). Ag+ exhibited the most significant adsorption, whereas Fe3+ showed limited removal, primarily due to precipitation at pH 7 rather than adsorption. Other metal ions displayed negligible adsorption under identical conditions, indicating good selectivity toward Ag+. It is speculated that Ag+, as a soft acid, exhibits strong affinity for soft base donor atoms such as nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups on the CPCDs surface (e.g., amines, imines, carboxyls). These functional groups facilitate stable coordination complexes and may also participate in redox reactions that reduce Ag+ to metallic Ag⁰, further enhancing adsorption through surface aggregation. This unexpected interaction prompted further evaluation on the Ag+ removal performance and potential applicability of CPCDs for water purification. We conducted adsorption isotherm and kinetic studies under varying initial Ag+ concentrations and time intervals.

The adsorption isotherm was evaluated at 298 K, and the experimental data were fitted using both Langmuir and Freundlich models. The adsorption capacity of CPCDs increased with increasing Ag+ concentration (Fig. 5
a). The maximum experimentally observed adsorption capacity reached approximately 710.2 mg g−1 at an initial Ag+ concentration of 30 g L−1. As shown in Fig. 5 b and Table S1, the Freundlich model provided a better correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.96) than the Langmuir model (R2 = 0.88), indicating that the adsorption process occurs on a heterogeneous surface through multilayer adsorption [26].

As shown in Fig. 5 c and d, the adsorption capacity increased rapidly during the initial stage, indicating abundant active sites on the CPCDs surface. Equilibrium was achieved within approximately 60 min. The removal efficiency of 300 mg/L Ag+ in the CPCDs solution exceeded 99 %. The kinetic data were analyzed using both pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models (Table S2). The pseudo-second-order model yielded a better fit (R2 = 0.998), implying that the adsorption rate is primarily governed by chemisorption [27–29]. The adsorption capacity derived from kinetic modeling was in agreement with the isotherm data, further supporting this conclusion. The combined results of isotherm modeling, kinetics, and spectroscopic analyses (e.g. FTIR, XPS) suggest a mechanism involving surface coordination and redox-induced aggregation, which drives the efficient quenching and rapid adsorption of Ag+ by CPCDs.

A comparative analysis with previously reported adsorbents was summarized in Table 3 . CPCDs were synthesized from biomass-derived CMC via a simple, green, and mild process, making it environmentally friendly and scalable. CPCDs showed rapid adsorption kinetics (equilibrium within 60 min) and a high adsorption capacity of 710.2 mg g−1. Additionally, CPCDs possessed dual functionality for both Ag+ adsorption and fluorescence-based detection. These features highlight the excellent potential of CPCDs for practical applications in selective Ag+ removal and monitoring in wastewater treatment.

In summary, this study highlights the potential of CPCDs as a novel, green, and efficient material for environmental pollution control. CPCDs with bright yellow fluorescence were synthesized in a one-step process under mild conditions using OPD and CMC as the raw material. The fluorescence of CPCDs was effectively quenched in the presence of Ag+, with a low detection limit of 0.01 μM. In addition, CPCDs showed remarkable Ag+ adsorption capabilities, reaching an equilibrium adsorption capacity of 710.2 mg/g. The adsorption process followed a pseudo-second-order kinetic model and was well-described by the Freundlich isotherm. The ability of CPCDs to simultaneously detect and remove Ag+ ions enhances their practical applicability for environmental remediation. The results provide a valuable foundation for further research into the development of multifunctional materials for heavy metal ion detection and removal.
Not applicable.
The article does not contain any experiments with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
Consent to participate was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.
Consent for publication was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126514.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
